enrichment.seo_title: involve.me Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: involve.me review 2026: an in-depth look at this no-code quiz, survey and funnel builder, its pricing tiers, key features, pros, cons and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: involve.me is a no-code interactive content and lead-generation platform that lets marketers, agencies and sales teams build quizzes, surveys, forms, calculators, landing pages, and embeddable pop-ups without writing code, then chain them together into multi-step conversion funnels using conditional branching logic. The company was co-founded and is led as CEO by Vlad Gozman, a Vienna-based entrepreneur who had previously co-founded AustrianStartups (2013), Adspired Technologies, maker of the analytics platform adverity (2014), and the VR content studio stereosense (2016). involve.me itself began life in 2018 as a content management system for virtual reality experiences; when Gozman and his team could not find sufficient market demand for the VR tool, they pivoted the underlying technology into an interactive-funnel builder and officially launched involve.me in April 2019 from Vienna, Austria, where the company remains headquartered today with a team in the 11-50 employee range. involve.me is bootstrapped rather than venture-funded (Crunchbase records only a small pre-seed round from March 2019), operates on a four-day work week, and by its own reporting has crossed seven-figure annual recurring revenue while serving more than 4,500 to 5,000 businesses worldwide, spanning marketing agencies, e-commerce brands, coaches, HR teams and SaaS companies. Technically, involve.me is a web-based drag-and-drop editor: users assemble 'funnels' from building blocks such as multiple-choice questions, scored quizzes, Net Promoter Score surveys, pricing calculators, product recommenders, and lead forms, then apply logic jumps so the path a visitor takes depends on their answers. Completed funnels can be embedded on any website, launched as standalone landing pages, or triggered as on-site pop-ups, and results flow into a built-in analytics dashboard or out to more than 30 third-party integrations, including Zapier, HubSpot, Mailchimp, ActiveCampaign, Google Sheets, Slack, and native webhooks. The platform ships with a library of 300-plus pre-built templates covering use cases like personality quizzes, ROI calculators, customer satisfaction surveys, and giveaway forms, and it layers in email automation workflows so a completed funnel can immediately trigger a nurture sequence. Pricing follows a classic SaaS ladder. The Free plan allows two live funnels, 50 monthly submissions and 500 monthly visits, with 30-day data retention and no access to logic jumps, integrations, or data export. Paid annual plans start with Start at $19 per month (3 live funnels, 1 user, over 50 premium features, 30-plus integrations), Grow at $49 per month (5 funnels, 2 users, branding removal, UTM tracking, one custom domain), and Scale at $119 per month (25 funnels, 5 users, A/B testing, custom CSS, webhooks); an Enterprise tier starts from $399 per month on annual contracts only and adds single sign-on, a security assessment and dedicated onboarding. Monthly (non-annual) billing runs higher, at $29, $69 and $139 respectively for Start, Grow and Scale. All paid tiers run on SOC 2 Type II-audited, GDPR-compliant infrastructure with 128-bit SSL encryption. involve.me's target audience is small-to-midsize marketing teams, agencies and solo creators who need to build lead-capture experiences quickly without engineering resources, competing most directly with Typeform (broader form-building brand but comparatively expensive and less funnel-focused), Outgrow (similar interactive-content and calculator focus but positioned more toward large enterprise), Jotform (forms-first, less oriented to marketing funnels), Interact (quiz-only niche), and Landbot or ConvertBox for conversational or pop-up-first lead capture. involve.me differentiates on its combined funnel-plus-automation workflow, breadth of ready-made templates, and aggressive freemium entry point aimed at capturing self-serve SMB signups before upselling into its mid-market paid tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: involve.me is a no-code SaaS platform built around a single idea: interactive content converts better than static landing pages. Instead of a plain contact form, businesses can build a scored quiz, an ROI calculator, or a multi-step survey that qualifies and segments visitors while collecting their contact details, then route each respondent down a different follow-up path based on how they answered.

The company was founded in Vienna, Austria by Vlad Gozman, who pivoted the product from an earlier, unsuccessful virtual-reality content tool into the interactive-funnel builder that launched in 2019. Since then involve.me has grown into a bootstrapped, profitable company serving thousands of businesses, from solo marketers to agencies running client campaigns, without taking on significant outside venture funding.

The platform is positioned squarely at marketing and growth teams rather than developers: everything from logic branching to integrations is configured through a visual editor, and the extensive template library is designed to get a funnel live within minutes rather than days.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the core of involve.me is its funnel builder, which lets users combine quizzes, surveys, forms and calculators into a single multi-step flow with conditional logic jumps, so different answers lead to different questions, outcomes or offers. Funnels can be embedded on an existing site, deployed as standalone pages, or launched as on-site pop-ups, giving marketers flexibility in how they insert interactive content into an existing funnel.

Beyond content creation, involve.me includes built-in email automation, so a completed quiz or form can immediately trigger a follow-up sequence, plus a results dashboard for tracking completion rates, drop-off points and lead quality. Higher tiers unlock A/B testing, custom CSS styling, custom domains, and webhooks, while more than 30 native integrations (Zapier, HubSpot, Mailchimp, ActiveCampaign, Google Sheets and Slack among them) connect captured leads to a business's existing marketing stack.

Security and compliance are notable for a tool at this price point: involve.me's paid plans run on SOC 2 Type II-audited, GDPR-compliant infrastructure with 128-bit SSL encryption, and the Enterprise tier adds single sign-on and a dedicated security assessment, making the platform usable by larger organizations with procurement requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: involve.me uses a freemium model. The Free plan supports two live funnels with 50 submissions and 500 visits per month, 30-day data retention, and access to the template library, but withholds integrations, logic jumps and data export. From there, annually billed plans start at Start ($19/month, 3 funnels, 1 user), move to Grow ($49/month, 5 funnels, 2 users, branding removal and a custom domain), and Scale ($119/month, 25 funnels, 5 users, A/B testing and webhooks), with an Enterprise tier starting from $399/month for custom limits, SSO and dedicated onboarding.

Monthly billing is available but costs noticeably more than the annual equivalent (for example Start is $29/month billed monthly versus $19/month billed annually), which is standard SaaS practice to reward annual commitments. Because pricing scales with both funnel count and team seats rather than raw traffic, small teams running a handful of funnels can typically stay on the Start or Grow tiers, while agencies managing many client campaigns tend to need Scale or a custom Enterprise agreement.

enrichment.seo_title: ioBroker Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ioBroker review 2026: a deep dive into this open-source home and building automation integration platform, its adapters, self-hosting setup and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ioBroker is a free, open-source integration platform for the Internet of Things that lets users connect and automate smart home devices, building automation systems, energy meters, and industrial process data from a single self-hosted server. Its roots trace back to CCU.IO, a popular tool in the German HomeMatic smart-home community, from which ioBroker emerged as a spin-off around 2014, developed primarily by Denis Haev, who goes by the online handle 'Bluefox' and remains the project's core maintainer, main developer and managing director of ioBroker GmbH, the small commercial entity that holds the ioBroker trademark and funds infrastructure such as the website, forum, and download servers. ioBroker GmbH operates with essentially one full-time employee (Bluefox himself) supported by a large volunteer contributor community, and is funded through a mix of external commercial development contracts and an optional paid cloud service, ioBroker.pro, rather than through venture capital. Architecturally, ioBroker is written in JavaScript and runs on Node.js, making it deployable on a Raspberry Pi, a Docker container, or any Linux, Windows or macOS machine with at least 1GB of RAM. Its core is the js-controller, a central coordinator process that manages a modular ecosystem of more than 1,000 community-built 'adapters' -- plugins that each speak to a specific device, protocol or service, covering smart home standards like KNX, Zigbee, Z-Wave and Homematic, cloud ecosystems like Amazon Alexa, Google Home and Sonos, and generic protocols like MQTT, Modbus and OPC-UA. Automation logic is configured through the built-in Admin web interface, a JavaScript-based scripting adapter (Blockly or native JS), and the VIS visualization module, which lets users build custom dashboards for monitoring and controlling connected devices without writing full applications. The bulk of ioBroker -- the js-controller and the vast majority of adapters -- is released as open-source software under the MIT license, meaning it can be installed, modified and run entirely for free and with full data ownership, since everything operates locally on the user's own hardware rather than through a third-party cloud. A handful of components require a free license issued by ioBroker GmbH for private, non-commercial use, primarily to track usage and fund upkeep of the wider project, while ioBroker.pro offers a paid, hosted convenience layer (remote access, backups and simplified server management) for users who don't want to self-host the underlying server. ioBroker's target audience is technically inclined home automation enthusiasts, hobbyists and small integrators, particularly within the German-speaking DIY smart-home community where it originated and remains most popular, though its adapter catalog and documentation have since expanded to a broader international, primarily English- and German-speaking user base. It competes most directly with Home Assistant, the dominant open-source home automation platform with a larger global community and Python-based architecture, as well as with openHAB, another Java-based open-source integration hub, and with proprietary hub ecosystems like Samsung SmartThings or Homey. ioBroker differentiates itself through its deep roots in and support for German building-automation and metering standards, its lightweight Node.js footprint suitable for low-powered hardware, and a governance model that keeps the core project free and self-hosted while funding development through a light commercial layer rather than advertising, data monetization or a hard paywall.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ioBroker is a self-hosted, open-source platform for tying together smart home devices, building automation systems and IoT data sources that would otherwise live in separate, incompatible apps and clouds. Rather than shipping its own hardware, ioBroker runs as software on hardware the user already owns, most commonly a Raspberry Pi, a small home server, or a Docker container, and talks to devices through a large library of community-maintained adapters.

The project grew out of CCU.IO, an earlier tool built for Germany's HomeMatic smart-home ecosystem, and emerged as its own platform around 2014 under the leadership of developer Denis Haev ('Bluefox'), who continues to drive core development today through the small company ioBroker GmbH. Rather than raising venture funding, the project sustains itself through a combination of contract development work, an optional paid cloud add-on, and community contributions.

Because it runs entirely on infrastructure the user controls, ioBroker appeals strongly to privacy-conscious users who want smart home automation without routing device data through a manufacturer's cloud, and to hobbyists who want to combine devices from many different ecosystems -- KNX, Zigbee, Z-Wave, MQTT and more -- into a single automation layer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the center of ioBroker is the js-controller, a Node.js process that manages the lifecycle of every installed adapter and routes data between them through a shared object and state database. Adapters exist for well over a thousand devices and services, spanning smart home protocols (Zigbee, Z-Wave, KNX, Homematic), voice assistants (Alexa, Google Home), media systems (Sonos, Philips Hue), and industrial or energy-monitoring protocols (Modbus, OPC-UA, MQTT), which is what lets ioBroker act as a universal translation layer across otherwise siloed ecosystems.

Automation and visualization are handled through companion tools: the JavaScript/Blockly scripting adapter lets users write conditional automation rules (for example, dim the lights when a motion sensor and a light-level sensor both fire), while the VIS module provides a drag-and-drop dashboard builder for creating custom control panels viewable from a browser or mobile device.

Administration happens through a web-based Admin interface for installing adapters, configuring instances, and monitoring logs, and the entire system supports multi-instance and multi-host setups, so power users can spread ioBroker across several machines for redundancy or to isolate resource-heavy adapters.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The ioBroker core (js-controller) and the overwhelming majority of its adapters are free and open source under the MIT license, so a self-hosted installation costs nothing beyond the hardware it runs on. A small number of adapters, particularly ones tied to commercial third-party integrations, require a free license issued by ioBroker GmbH for private use, which is used mainly to gauge adoption rather than to charge users.

For users who don't want to manage their own server, ioBroker GmbH offers ioBroker.pro, a paid hosted/cloud convenience service that handles remote access and backups for a subscription fee. This keeps the underlying platform's cost model close to zero for self-hosters while giving the maintaining company a modest, sustainable revenue stream to fund ongoing development, distinct from a typical SaaS pricing ladder.

faqs.answer: Yes, the ioBroker core and the vast majority of its adapters are free and open source under the MIT license. An optional paid ioBroker.pro cloud service is available for users who don't want to self-host.

faqs.question: What hardware does ioBroker run on?
faqs.answer: ioBroker runs on any device that supports Node.js with at least 1GB of RAM, including a Raspberry Pi, Docker container, Linux server, Windows PC or Mac.

faqs.question: Who develops ioBroker?
faqs.answer: ioBroker is maintained by developer Denis Haev ('Bluefox') through ioBroker GmbH, alongside a large community of volunteer adapter contributors.

faqs.question: How is ioBroker different from Home Assistant?
faqs.answer: Both are open-source, self-hosted home automation platforms, but ioBroker is built on Node.js/JavaScript with especially strong support for German building-automation standards like KNX and Homematic, while Home Assistant is Python-based with a larger global community.

faqs.question: Does ioBroker support Zigbee and Z-Wave?
faqs.answer: Yes, ioBroker has dedicated adapters for Zigbee and Z-Wave, alongside KNX, Homematic, MQTT, Modbus and many other protocols.

faqs.question: Can ioBroker control voice assistants like Alexa?
faqs.answer: Yes, ioBroker includes adapters for Amazon Alexa and Google Home, allowing voice control of connected devices.

enrichment.seo_title: iRedMail Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: iRedMail review 2026: an in-depth look at this open-source mail server installer, its architecture, editions, pricing and top self-hosted alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: iRedMail is a free, open-source shell-script installer that assembles a complete, production-ready mail server on a single Linux or BSD machine by combining and pre-configuring a stack of well-known open-source mail components, so administrators don't have to manually integrate Postfix, Dovecot, a directory or database backend, webmail, and anti-spam tooling from scratch. The project was started in November 2007 by developer Zhang Huangbin, who remains its primary author and maintainer, and who built iRedMail to solve the recurring pain of manually wiring together a mail server's many moving parts -- a process that historically took experienced sysadmins days to get right and secure. Architecturally, iRedMail deploys Postfix as the SMTP transport, Dovecot for POP3/IMAP, and gives administrators a choice of backend for accounts and domains: OpenLDAP, MySQL/MariaDB, or PostgreSQL. On top of that it layers Roundcube webmail and/or the SOGo groupware suite (for shared calendars, address books and tasks via CalDAV/CardDAV, plus ActiveSync for mobile), Amavisd, SpamAssassin and ClamAV for anti-spam and anti-virus filtering, OpenDKIM and SPF for outbound authentication, Fail2ban for intrusion prevention, and iRedAPD as a Postfix policy daemon for advanced sender restrictions. All mail-in-transit is enforced over TLS, and the finished install is designed to be 'secure by default,' with SSHA512/BCRYPT password hashing and mail quarantine built in. iRedMail supports mainstream Linux and BSD distributions including CentOS Stream, Rocky Linux, AlmaLinux, Debian, Ubuntu, FreeBSD and OpenBSD, and is released and distributed under the GPLv3 open-source license, meaning the core installer and resulting mail server are entirely free to download, run, and modify, with unlimited domains, users and mailing lists. The project sustains itself commercially through two optional paid products layered on top of the free base: iRedAdmin-Pro, a full-featured web-based administration panel (the free edition bundled with iRedMail is deliberately limited) priced as a license with an annual renewal fee reported around $250/year for continued updates and support, and iRedMail Easy, a web-based deployment and support platform that helps less experienced administrators stand up and maintain their own iRedMail server, priced at roughly $499/year or about $49/month on a 12-month plan. Because everything runs on infrastructure the customer owns, there is no per-mailbox SaaS fee and no vendor lock-in, which is the core value proposition against hosted email services. iRedMail's target audience is systems administrators, small businesses, hosting providers, privacy-focused organizations and self-hosting enthusiasts who want full ownership of their email infrastructure and data rather than relying on Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, or a managed IMAP host. Its direct open-source competitors include Mail-in-a-Box, Mailcow, Modoboa, and Zimbra's community edition, as well as the older Postfix Admin project, with iRedMail differentiating itself through its especially broad distribution support, choice of LDAP/SQL backend, long track record dating back to 2007, and its optional paid support tier for organizations that want the reliability of a maintained open-source stack without paying recurring per-seat SaaS pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: iRedMail solves a specific, long-standing sysadmin problem: standing up a secure, full-featured mail server from open-source parts normally requires manually integrating a dozen separate projects -- an SMTP daemon, an IMAP server, a spam filter, an antivirus scanner, a webmail client and an authentication backend -- each with its own configuration quirks and security pitfalls. iRedMail packages that entire stack into a single guided installer script that produces a working, secured mail server in minutes rather than days.

Started in 2007 by developer Zhang Huangbin, the project has been continuously maintained for nearly two decades and supports a wide range of Linux and BSD distributions, making it one of the longest-running self-hosted mail server projects still in active development.

The base installer and resulting server are fully open source and free under GPLv3, with the project's commercial layer limited to an optional, more advanced admin panel and an optional managed-deployment support service, rather than gating the mail server itself behind a paywall.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: iRedMail's core stack pairs Postfix for SMTP with Dovecot for POP3/IMAP, backed by the administrator's choice of OpenLDAP, MySQL/MariaDB, or PostgreSQL for storing domains, users and mailing lists, giving organizations flexibility to match their existing directory infrastructure. On top of that base, Roundcube webmail and the SOGo groupware suite provide browser-based email, plus CalDAV/CardDAV calendars and contacts and ActiveSync for syncing with mobile devices.

Security is handled through a layered stack: Amavisd, SpamAssassin and ClamAV filter spam and malware, OpenDKIM and SPF authenticate outbound mail, Fail2ban blocks brute-force login attempts, and iRedAPD enforces custom Postfix sender/recipient policies. All client connections are forced over TLS by default, and captured spam is routed into a quarantine rather than silently dropped.

For administrators who need more than the bundled free admin panel offers, iRedAdmin-Pro adds features like per-domain reporting, advanced mailbox quota management, and a more polished management UI, while iRedMail Easy provides a hosted deployment assistant that walks less experienced users through provisioning and maintaining their own self-hosted server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core iRedMail installer, and the full mail server it produces (unlimited domains, users, and mailing lists on Postfix, Dovecot, Roundcube/SOGo and the full anti-spam/antivirus stack) is completely free and open source under GPLv3, with no license fee for self-hosting on the administrator's own server.

Two optional paid products sit on top: iRedAdmin-Pro, the advanced admin panel, is sold as a license with a reported renewal fee around $250 per year for continued updates, and iRedMail Easy, a hosted setup and support platform aimed at less technical administrators, runs about $499 per year or $49 per month on a 12-month plan. Neither product is required to run a fully functional iRedMail server -- they exist purely to simplify administration or provide additional support for organizations willing to pay for it.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ironclad review 2026: a detailed look at this AI-powered contract lifecycle management platform, its features, pricing and top CLM alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ironclad is an enterprise contract lifecycle management (CLM) platform that helps legal, sales, procurement and other business teams create, negotiate, sign, store and analyze contracts throughout their entire lifecycle from a single system of record. The company was founded in 2014 by Jason Boehmig, a former corporate attorney at Fenwick and West, and Cai GoGwilt, a software engineer, who built Ironclad after Boehmig experienced firsthand how manual, email- and paper-based contracting slowed businesses down; the company went through Y Combinator's 2015 batch and opened its first office in San Francisco's SOMA neighborhood with the stated ambition of building 'the best CLM in SOMA.' Ironclad is headquartered in San Francisco with an additional office in New York, and has grown to roughly 500 to 850-plus employees and more than 1,000 customers, with the company reporting it has processed over 2 billion contracts as of 2025. In May 2025, Ironclad brought on Dan Springer, previously CEO of DocuSign and Responsys, as its new chief executive, succeeding co-founder Boehmig in that role as the company pushes further into AI-driven contracting. Ironclad has raised a total of roughly $333 million in venture funding across five rounds: an $8 million Series A led by Accel in 2017, a $23 million Series B led by Sequoia Capital in 2018, a $50 million Series C led by Y Combinator's Continuity fund in 2019, a $100 million Series D led by Mary Meeker's BOND in 2021, and a $150 million Series E funded entirely by existing investors in January 2022, which valued the company at $3.2 billion, making it one of the more richly valued private legal-tech companies. Technically, Ironclad is a cloud-based SaaS platform built around a structured contract repository with searchable metadata, a no-code workflow designer for building approval and negotiation processes, native e-signature (alongside integrations with DocuSign and other e-sign tools), and Ironclad Clickwrap for high-volume, click-to-accept agreements like terms of service or NDAs. Its AI layer, marketed under names including Ironclad AI Assist and an AI legal copilot sometimes referred to as Jurist, uses generative AI to redline contracts against playbooks, extract key terms and obligations from a repository, flag risk, and answer natural-language questions about a company's contract portfolio; the platform also includes analytics and reporting dashboards and pre-built integrations with Salesforce, Slack, Microsoft, and other business systems, backed by SOC 2 compliance. Ironclad does not publish self-serve pricing; the platform is sold via a direct sales process with quote-based tiers commonly described in the market as Standard, Professional and Enterprise, aimed respectively at small legal teams (roughly 5 to 15 users), mid-market companies (15 to 50 users), and large enterprises (50-plus users) needing unlimited contract volume and dedicated account management. Third-party pricing-benchmark data puts typical annual contract values in the $30,000 to $250,000-plus range, with a reported median purchase price near $39,995 per year and a minimum annual contract around $15,000, while Ironclad's separate AI Assist module is reported to carry additional pricing on top of the base platform. Ironclad's target customers are mid-market to enterprise legal, sales operations and procurement teams that have outgrown shared drives, email approvals and generic e-signature tools. Its principal competitors include DocuSign CLM, Icertis, ContractPodAi, Agiloft, SirionLabs, Concord and PandaDoc, with Ironclad generally differentiating on its workflow-first, no-code contract automation design, strong integration ecosystem, and early, aggressive investment in generative AI features for contract drafting and analysis.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ironclad is built to replace the patchwork of email threads, shared drives and static templates that most legal and business teams historically used to manage contracts, offering instead a single structured repository where every contract's metadata, clauses, approvals and signature status are tracked and searchable. The platform is aimed primarily at in-house legal teams but extends workflow access to sales, procurement and other business functions that originate or need visibility into contracts.

Founded in 2014 by attorney Jason Boehmig and engineer Cai GoGwilt and launched out of Y Combinator in 2015, Ironclad has grown from a small San Francisco startup into a well-funded CLM category leader, having raised roughly $333 million across five funding rounds and reaching a $3.2 billion valuation by its Series E in 2022.

With over 1,000 customers and a leadership change in 2025 that brought in former DocuSign CEO Dan Springer, Ironclad continues to push further into generative AI for contracting, positioning itself against both legacy document-management vendors and newer AI-native contracting startups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Ironclad's foundation is its structured contract repository, which stores every contract with searchable metadata and links related documents, amendments and obligations together rather than treating each contract as an isolated file. On top of the repository sits a no-code workflow designer that lets legal and business teams build custom intake, approval and negotiation workflows without engineering support, alongside native e-signature and Ironclad Clickwrap for high-volume click-to-accept agreements like terms of service.

The platform's AI layer applies generative AI to contract review and drafting, redlining incoming paper against a company's preferred playbook language, extracting key terms and obligations across the repository, and surfacing risk before a human reviewer sees the document, reducing the manual review burden on legal teams.

Ironclad also provides analytics dashboards for tracking cycle time, bottlenecks and contract volume, plus pre-built integrations with systems like Salesforce, Slack and Microsoft so that contract requests and approvals can originate from tools business teams already use, backed by SOC 2 compliance for enterprise security requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ironclad does not publish self-serve pricing and sells exclusively through a direct sales process, with tiers commonly discussed in the market as Standard, Professional and Enterprise, scaling from small legal teams of roughly 5-15 users up to enterprise deployments of 50-plus users with unlimited contract volume and dedicated account management.

Independent pricing-benchmark sources report typical annual contract values ranging from about $30,000 to well over $250,000 depending on company size and modules purchased, with a median reported purchase price near $39,995 per year and a minimum annual contract around $15,000; Ironclad's separately priced AI Assist module and complex system integrations can add substantially to total first-year cost.

enrichment.seo_title: Islandora Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Islandora review 2026: a deep dive into this open-source digital repository framework built on Drupal and Fedora, its features, governance and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Islandora is a free, open-source digital asset management and repository framework used primarily by libraries, archives, museums and other cultural heritage and research institutions to ingest, preserve, describe and provide public access to digitized collections such as scanned manuscripts, photographs, audio, video and research datasets. The project originated in 2006 at the University of Prince Edward Island's Robertson Library in Canada, where library staff, led by figures including Mark Leggott, built the first version to manage the university's own growing digital collections; the software was subsequently open-sourced and adopted by other institutions, and the university's technology transfer led to the founding of discoverygarden inc., a commercial support and hosting company for Islandora based in Charlottetown, PEI. As adoption spread beyond UPEI, governance of the project transitioned to the community, and today Islandora is stewarded by the Islandora Foundation, a member-supported non-profit organization that coordinates roadmap decisions, releases and community events (including the annual Islandoracon conference) on behalf of the global institutions running the software, rather than by any single vendor. Technically, Islandora is a 'glue' framework that combines several independent open-source systems rather than reinventing them: Drupal serves as the content management and user-facing interaction layer, Fedora Commons acts as the underlying repository that stores digital objects and their preservation metadata, and Apache Solr powers full-text and faceted search and discovery across the collection. The current major version, Islandora 8 (branded simply 'Islandora' going forward), rebuilt the stack on modern Drupal (9/10), Fedora 6, and a set of decoupled microservices known as Crayfish that handle tasks like image derivative generation via a Cantaloupe IIIF image server, audio/video processing, and OCR, communicating over an ActiveMQ message queue; this modular, IIIF-compliant architecture lets institutions plug in high-resolution zoomable image viewers and time-based media players for archival AV content. Because Islandora is entirely open source (its components carry standard open-source licenses such as the GPL and MIT, consistent with Drupal's licensing), there is no software cost to download, install or modify it; the Islandora Foundation instead sustains itself through a tiered membership model -- Member, Collaborator and Partner classes, alongside a contributor program for individuals and institutions unable to pay dues -- with suggested annual contributions historically in the hundreds of dollars for community-affiliated groups, while institutions that want hosting, migration or custom development support typically contract with third-party vendors such as discoverygarden, LYRASIS, or Common Media rather than paying Islandora itself. Islandora's core audience is academic and public libraries, museums, archives and government agencies running institutional repositories, digital library programs or born-digital preservation initiatives, particularly organizations that already use or are comfortable with Drupal. Its main competitors in the digital repository and digital asset management space include DSpace (a similarly open-source institutional repository platform popular for research output), the Samvera/Hyrax framework (a Ruby-on-Rails-based repository system with a similar community-governance model), ArchivesSpace for archival description, CONTENTdm from OCLC, and commercial platforms like Preservica and Ex Libris Rosetta; Islandora differentiates itself through its tight integration with Drupal's flexible content-management and theming capabilities, its IIIF-native image and AV delivery, and its fully community-governed, vendor-neutral open-source model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Islandora exists to solve a problem specific to libraries, archives and museums: how to ingest, describe, preserve and publicly expose large, heterogeneous digital collections -- scanned books, photographs, audio and video recordings, and research datasets -- in a system built for long-term stewardship rather than short-lived web content. Rather than building a monolithic application, Islandora combines Drupal, the Fedora Commons repository, and Apache Solr search into a single integrated framework.

The project began in 2006 at the University of Prince Edward Island's Robertson Library, where it was created to manage the university's own digital collections before being released as open source and adopted by institutions worldwide. Governance has since moved from the originating university to the community-run, member-supported Islandora Foundation, which coordinates the project's roadmap, releases and annual Islandoracon conference.

Because Islandora is fully open source, institutions can install and run it without any license fee, though most rely on either in-house technical staff or third-party vendors such as discoverygarden for hosting, migration and ongoing support, reflecting the project's community-governed, vendor-neutral model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Islandora's architecture layers Drupal as the content management and public-facing interaction layer on top of Fedora Commons, which handles the actual storage and preservation metadata for digital objects, with Apache Solr providing fast full-text and faceted search across the collection. The current Islandora release rebuilt this on modern Drupal 9/10 and Fedora 6, connected through a set of decoupled 'Crayfish' microservices for tasks like image and audio/video derivative generation and OCR.

IIIF (International Image Interoperability Framework) support is built in via a Cantaloupe image server, allowing institutions to deliver high-resolution, zoomable images and interoperable viewers for manuscripts, maps and photographs, while dedicated audio and video handling supports born-digital and digitized time-based media collections.

Because it inherits Drupal's content-management capabilities, Islandora also offers flexible metadata modeling (supporting standards like MODS and Dublin Core), customizable theming, granular access control for restricted collections, and batch ingest tools for importing large digitization backlogs, all of which are extensible through Drupal's broader module ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Islandora itself carries no license fee; the software, its Drupal modules, and its Crayfish microservices are all open source and free to download, install and modify. There is no tiered commercial pricing structure for the software the way there would be for a proprietary digital asset management platform.

Sustainability instead comes through the Islandora Foundation's membership program, which offers Member, Collaborator and Partner tiers of financial support for institutions that can afford to contribute, alongside a contributor program aimed at including organizations that cannot pay dues. Institutions that want hands-on implementation, hosting or migration support typically contract separately with third-party vendors like discoverygarden or LYRASIS rather than paying Islandora directly.

enrichment.short_description: Lightweight, self-hosted commenting server written in Python that serves as a privacy-friendly, open-source alternative to Disqus.
enrichment.seo_title: Isso Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Isso review 2026: an in-depth look at this self-hosted, open-source Disqus alternative for blog comments, its features, setup and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Isso is a lightweight, self-hosted commenting server designed as a drop-in, privacy-respecting alternative to Disqus and similar third-party comment widgets. Its name is short for the German phrase 'Ich schrei sonst,' meaning 'I'll scream otherwise,' reflecting the project's origin as developer Martin Zimmermann's (GitHub handle 'posativ') personal project to add comments to his own static blog without handing visitor data to a third-party service. Isso's earliest publicly tracked package releases on PyPI date to 2014, though the project's origins as an independent open-source tool trace earlier, and it has since been carried forward by the open-source community under the isso-comments GitHub organization after moving from Zimmermann's original posativ/isso repository. Isso is released under the MIT license, making it free to download, self-host and modify, and it was purpose-built for exactly the audience Disqus and similar hosted comment platforms don't serve well: owners of static site generators like Jekyll, Hugo, Pelican and Hexo who want commenting functionality without embedding third-party trackers, ads or analytics scripts on their pages. Technically, Isso is a server application written in Python (requiring Python 3.8 or later) paired with a compact JavaScript client, and it stores all comment data locally in a SQLite database rather than sending it to any external service, meaning every comment, IP address and moderation action stays entirely on the operator's own infrastructure. The included JavaScript client is deliberately minimal, weighing around 65 kilobytes uncompressed (about 20 kilobytes gzipped), a fraction of the footprint of most hosted comment widgets, which helps keep page load times fast on lightweight, static-generated blogs. Functionally, Isso supports Markdown-formatted comments, allows commenters to edit or delete their own comments within a configurable window (15 minutes by default), includes moderation tools for site owners to approve, reject or delete comments, and ships with built-in import tools for migrating existing comment threads out of Disqus, WordPress and other platforms, removing a major switching-cost barrier for bloggers who want to leave a hosted service. Because Isso has no company behind it and no commercial tier, there is no pricing model beyond the cost of the server infrastructure an operator chooses to run it on, whether that's a small VPS, a Docker container, or a shared web host with Python support; typical operating cost is a few dollars a month for hosting rather than any software license fee. Isso's target audience is developers, technical bloggers and small publishers running static or lightweight dynamic sites who want commenting without outsourcing visitor data or page weight to a third party, and who are comfortable deploying and maintaining a small Python service themselves. Its direct competitors in the self-hosted commenting space include Commento (and its fork Commento++), Remark42, Cusdis, and the federated, ActivityPub-based Comentario and Coral by Vox Media, as well as static-first solutions like Staticman and Utterances (which stores comments as GitHub issues); against hosted incumbents like Disqus and Commento's own hosted tier, Isso differentiates itself primarily through its minimal footprint, complete data ownership via local SQLite storage, and its long-standing, no-frills focus on being a simple, dependable comment backend rather than a full engagement or analytics platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Isso fills a specific niche: giving blog and static site owners a way to accept reader comments without routing visitor data through a third-party advertising and analytics company like Disqus. It runs as a small, self-hosted Python server paired with a lightweight JavaScript widget that any site can embed, regardless of what the rest of the site is built with.

The project was created by developer Martin Zimmermann, known online as 'posativ,' originally to serve his own blog's needs, and has since been carried forward by the open-source community, with the codebase now maintained under the isso-comments GitHub organization. It has no corporate backer and no paid tier; it is simply a free, open-source tool maintained by contributors.

Isso is particularly popular among users of static site generators such as Jekyll, Hugo and Pelican, which have no native way to accept dynamic comments and would otherwise need to rely on a hosted third-party widget.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Isso is a Python server backed by SQLite, meaning every comment, reply and moderation decision is stored in a single local database file the site owner fully controls, with no data ever leaving their own server. The accompanying JavaScript client is intentionally minimal, at roughly 65KB uncompressed, keeping page weight low compared to most hosted comment widgets.

Isso supports Markdown formatting in comments, lets commenters edit or delete their own submissions within a configurable time window (15 minutes by default), and gives site administrators a moderation queue to approve, reject or remove comments before or after they go live, helping control spam and abuse without an external moderation service.

To ease migration away from hosted platforms, Isso includes built-in importers for Disqus and WordPress comment exports, so site owners switching away from a third-party service can bring their existing comment history along rather than starting from zero.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Isso itself is entirely free and open source under the MIT license; there is no license fee, subscription, or paid tier of any kind, since the project has no company or commercial arm behind it. Anyone can download the source from GitHub or install it via PyPI at no cost.

The only ongoing cost is whatever server infrastructure an operator chooses to run it on, since Isso must be self-hosted rather than used as a managed cloud service. In practice this typically means a small virtual private server or Docker container costing a few dollars a month, a fraction of what a hosted comment platform with a paid tier would charge, especially for sites with any meaningful comment volume.

enrichment.seo_title: Iterable Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Iterable review 2026: a detailed look at this AI-powered cross-channel marketing platform, its features, pricing tiers and top alternatives like Braze.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Iterable is an enterprise customer engagement and cross-channel marketing platform that lets brands orchestrate personalized campaigns and automated customer journeys across email, SMS, push notifications, in-app messages, web and direct mail from a single system. The company was founded in San Francisco in 2013 by Justin Zhu, a former Twitter engineer, and Andrew Boni, a former Google engineer, who built Iterable to give marketing teams the kind of granular, data-driven, cross-channel orchestration that had previously required stitching together separate point tools for each channel. Iterable has raised a total of roughly $342 million in venture funding, with its largest round a $200 million Series E in June 2021 led by Silver Lake and Adams Street Partners, a raise that pushed the company's valuation to around $2 billion and made it a unicorn; Iterable remains a private company as of 2026, with no announced IPO timeline, and has grown to an employee base in the 500 to 1,000-plus range spread across offices on multiple continents. Technically, Iterable is built around a customer data-driven architecture: marketers upload or sync user and event data (often via a customer data platform, data warehouse, or direct API/SDK integration), build segments from that data, and design multi-step customer journeys in Studio, Iterable's visual, drag-and-drop workflow canvas that supports event-triggered automation, branching logic, time-based scheduling and A/B/multivariate testing. Campaigns and journeys can span email, SMS, mobile and web push notifications, in-app messages, and direct mail, all coordinated from the same platform so a customer's experience stays consistent regardless of channel. In 2025 and 2026 Iterable significantly expanded its generative AI capabilities under the 'Nova Intelligence' banner, including Nova Agent, an autonomous AI agent that can build, QA and optimize campaigns with reduced manual input; Brand Affinity scoring, which predicts each individual user's preferred channel and content type; Predictive Goals, which forecasts churn risk and conversion likelihood per user; Send Time Optimization; and AI-assisted copywriting, alongside a 2026-era Iterable Command Center that gives marketers a centralized, goal-based view of campaign performance across the whole program. Iterable does not publish self-serve pricing; the platform is sold through an enterprise sales process with pricing based primarily on monthly active users (MAUs) contacted across channels, along with which channels (email, SMS, push, in-app) and features a customer licenses. Publicly discussed tiers include Growth, Premier and Enterprise, with Growth aimed at teams just starting cross-channel marketing (core channels, basic segmentation and workflow automation) and Premier/Enterprise unlocking additional channels, advanced AI features and higher support levels; third-party pricing-benchmark sources estimate typical costs from roughly $36,000 to $72,000 per year for entry-level/SMB deployments, $72,000 to $180,000 for mid-market, and $200,000-plus for large enterprise programs, with SMS and push often carrying additional usage-based fees on top of the base MAU pricing. Iterable's target customers are mid-market to enterprise marketing teams at consumer brands in retail, media, gaming, fintech and travel that need to run sophisticated, data-rich, multi-channel lifecycle marketing programs rather than simple batch-and-blast email campaigns. Its most direct competitors are Braze (its closest rival in mobile-first, enterprise cross-channel engagement), Klaviyo (stronger in e-commerce/Shopify-centric email and SMS), Salesforce Marketing Cloud (broader CRM-integrated suite), Airship and MoEngage (mobile engagement specialists), and Customer.io (mid-market-focused workflow automation); Iterable differentiates itself through its emphasis on marketer-controlled, data-driven segmentation without requiring heavy engineering support, combined with its aggressive recent investment in generative AI for autonomous campaign building and per-user channel/content personalization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Iterable positions itself as a unified control center for customer engagement, replacing the separate email service provider, push notification tool, SMS platform and in-app messaging system that many marketing teams historically stitched together. Every channel is driven from the same underlying customer data and the same visual journey builder, so a single automated program can email a user, follow up with a push notification if they don't open it, and escalate to SMS if neither lands.

The company was founded in San Francisco in 2013 by former Twitter engineer Justin Zhu and former Google engineer Andrew Boni, and grew into a well-funded category leader, raising roughly $342 million in venture capital and reaching unicorn status with a roughly $2 billion valuation after its 2021 Series E led by Silver Lake.

Iterable remains privately held as of 2026, with several hundred to just over a thousand employees globally, and has increasingly positioned generative AI, branded as Nova Intelligence, as its primary differentiator against rivals like Braze and Klaviyo.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Iterable's core is Studio, a visual, drag-and-drop journey builder that lets marketers design event-triggered, branching customer journeys with built-in A/B and multivariate testing, spanning email, SMS, mobile and web push, in-app messaging and direct mail from one interface. Campaigns pull directly from synced customer and event data, allowing highly granular audience segmentation without engineering involvement for most day-to-day campaign work.

The platform's Nova Intelligence AI suite adds Nova Agent for autonomous campaign building, QA and optimization; Brand Affinity scoring that predicts each user's preferred channel and content; Predictive Goals for forecasting churn and conversion likelihood; Send Time Optimization; and AI-assisted copywriting, collectively aimed at reducing manual campaign configuration while improving per-user personalization.

A 2026-era addition, Iterable Command Center, gives marketing leaders a centralized, goal-based performance view across every active campaign and journey, while the platform's broader ecosystem includes integrations with customer data platforms, data warehouses like Snowflake, and CRM systems like Salesforce for keeping customer data continuously in sync.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Iterable does not offer self-serve, publicly listed pricing; the platform is sold through an enterprise sales process with cost driven primarily by monthly active users (MAUs) contacted across channels, plus which channels and AI features a customer licenses. Publicly referenced tiers include Growth, Premier and Enterprise, moving from core email/SMS/push functionality up to the full channel and AI feature set with higher-touch support.

Third-party benchmark data suggests entry-level and SMB deployments typically run from about $36,000 to $72,000 per year, mid-market programs from roughly $72,000 to $180,000, and large enterprise programs $200,000 and up, with SMS and push notification usage frequently carrying additional per-message or per-user fees layered on top of the base MAU-driven subscription.

enrichment.seo_title: iTop Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: iTop review 2026: an in-depth look at this open-source ITSM and CMDB platform by Combodo, its features, editions, pricing and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: iTop, short for 'IT Operations Portal,' is an open-source IT service management (ITSM) and configuration management database (CMDB) platform developed by Combodo, a software company founded in 2010 in the Grenoble region of France (based in Echirolles) by three co-founders: Erwan Taloc, Denis Flaven and Romain Quetiez. Combodo built iTop as a web-based, ITIL-aligned platform that lets IT teams manage their entire operational stack -- incidents, problems, changes, service requests and the underlying configuration items that make up their IT environment -- from a single, self-hostable application, and has grown the product to more than 2,900 organizations using it worldwide as of recent company reporting, while Combodo itself remains a relatively small company of roughly 11 to 50 employees. Technically, iTop combines a fully customizable CMDB (which auto-discovers and tracks servers, applications, network devices and their interdependencies) with an ITIL-oriented service desk covering incident management, problem management, change management, and a self-service portal for end users to submit and track requests, plus a built-in knowledge base and SLA management for tracking response and resolution commitments. A distinguishing feature is iTop's low-code data model designer, which lets administrators customize or extend the underlying object model (adding new configuration item classes, custom fields or relationships) without needing to write PHP code, alongside a REST/JSON web services API for integrating with monitoring tools, directory services (LDAP/SSO), and other IT systems. The community/core edition of iTop is released as free and open-source software, distributed under the AGPL-3.0 license (the GNU Affero General Public License), meaning the source is publicly available on GitHub, can be freely self-hosted, and any modified version made available over a network must also share its source, which Combodo has said is deliberately structured to prevent the software from being closed off by third-party resellers. On top of the free Community edition (recommended for teams of roughly 10 or fewer agents, available on-premise), Combodo sells two paid editions: iTop Atelier, aimed at growing teams (up to roughly 50 agents) needing an ITIL-tailored, operationally supported deployment available both on-premise and as SaaS, and iTop Forge, aimed at large organizations (50-plus agents) that need a fully industrialized, deeply customized deployment with expert support, also available on-premise or as SaaS; neither Atelier's nor Forge's pricing is published, and interested customers must contact Combodo directly for a quote. iTop's extensibility is further supported by 'iTop Hub,' a marketplace/community toolset of add-ons, extensions and integrations built by Combodo and third-party contributors that plug into the core platform. iTop's target audience is IT operations and support teams, from small internal IT departments running the free Community edition to managed service providers (MSPs) and larger enterprises that adopt Atelier or Forge for multi-tenant, ITIL-certified service management with vendor support. Its main competitors include GLPI (another French open-source ITSM/asset-management tool, iTop's closest direct rival), ServiceNow and Jira Service Management (enterprise-grade, much higher-cost proprietary platforms), SysAid, and Ivanti Neurons for ITSM; iTop differentiates itself through its combination of a genuinely free, unrestricted open-source core, deep CMDB customization via its low-code data model designer, and its France-based, ITIL-focused development team, appealing especially to European organizations and MSPs that want data sovereignty and full source-code transparency without sacrificing enterprise ITSM functionality.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: iTop is designed to give IT operations teams a single, web-based system for tracking both what their IT environment consists of (through its CMDB) and how they operate and support it (through ITIL-aligned incident, problem, change and request management). Rather than treating asset tracking and service desk ticketing as separate tools, iTop ties them together so, for example, an incident ticket can be directly linked to the specific server or application configuration item it affects.

The platform is developed by Combodo, a French software company founded in Grenoble in 2010 by Erwan Taloc, Denis Flaven and Romain Quetiez, and now based in Echirolles near Grenoble. Combodo has grown iTop to more than 2,900 organizations worldwide while remaining a relatively small, focused team of roughly 11 to 50 employees.

iTop's core is released under the AGPL-3.0 open-source license and can be freely downloaded, self-hosted and modified, with Combodo generating revenue instead from paid, supported editions (Atelier and Forge) and professional services for organizations that want a managed or heavily customized deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the heart of iTop is its CMDB, which tracks IT assets -- servers, applications, network devices, and the relationships and dependencies between them -- giving support teams visibility into what an incident or change might impact before they act. This CMDB is fully customizable through iTop's low-code data model designer, which lets administrators add new object classes, fields and relationships without writing PHP code.

On the service management side, iTop covers the core ITIL processes: incident management, problem management, change management, and a self-service portal where end users can submit and track their own requests, backed by a knowledge base and configurable SLA tracking for measuring response and resolution performance against targets.

Extensibility comes through a REST/JSON API for integrating with monitoring tools, LDAP directories and SSO providers, plus 'iTop Hub,' a community and Combodo-maintained marketplace of add-ons and extensions that plug additional functionality -- from specific integrations to industry-specific workflows -- into the core platform without forking the codebase.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: iTop Community, the free open-source edition, is unrestricted and recommended for teams of roughly 10 or fewer agents running the platform on-premise; there is no license fee and no artificial feature-gating built into the free tier. This makes iTop one of the few genuinely full-featured, no-cost ITSM platforms available for small IT teams.

For larger deployments, Combodo sells iTop Atelier (targeting teams up to roughly 50 agents, available on-premise or as SaaS) and iTop Forge (targeting 50-plus agents needing deep customization and industrialized support, also on-premise or SaaS), but neither edition's pricing is published; both require contacting Combodo directly for a custom quote based on agent count, deployment model and support level.
